title: Lesson 5 Overview
subtitle: Listen to the lesson explained in English

transcript.en: Welcome to lesson five, Luis. This lesson teaches fruit, vegetables, and two grammar pieces that unlock most everyday Maltese conversation: the imperative — giving orders or instructions — and the present tense.
First, fruit and vegetables. Maltese nouns for fruit and veg often come in three forms, which is unusual to English speakers but really useful once you see the pattern. Take apple. One apple is
Apple as a type of fruit, in general — what you'd call a collective — is

So when someone offers you 'an apple' the singular tuffieħa is right. When you talk about 'apples' as a category, like 'I love apples', you use the collective tuffieħ. When you mean a specific number — 'three apples in this bowl' — you use the plural tuffiħat.
The same three-form pattern works for grapes:
And for vegetables it's the same. Onion is
Don't try to memorise all sixteen fruits and sixteen vegetables now — the cards walk you through them one by one with audio.

Now grammar. The imperative is the form you use to give a command, request, warning or piece of advice. It has two forms: singular for one person, plural for several people. So 'walk' to one person is
There's a useful trick: take the present tense form and drop the first letter. The verb 'I understand' is
Drop the n and you get the imperative — 'understand'!
It works the other way too. Once you know the imperative, you can build the present tense by adding a prefix.
Now the present tense. Maltese verbs change their prefix depending on who's doing the action. The pattern uses the imperative as the base.
If the imperative starts with a vowel — like oħroġ meaning 'go out' — the prefixes are: n for I, t for you, j for he, t again for she, n for we, t for you-plural, j for they. So 'I go out' is
If the imperative starts with a consonant — like poġġi meaning 'sit' — the prefixes shift slightly: in or n for I, t for you, i or j for he. So 'I sit' is
Once you've got the imperative, the present tense almost builds itself. The exercises will conjugate two verbs in full — xorob, to drink, and ħarab, to escape — so the pattern locks in.
That's lesson five. The fruit and vegetable cards will teach the three forms; the imperative and present sections drill the verbs. Saħħa.
